transcript.content: Dmitri, do you know roughly what time you took the pills — like was it all at once or spread out over a few hours?
Yeah, it was all at once. I just... I took them all at the same time, maybe around 2 in the morning. So it's been like six hours since then, give or take.
Did you take anything else tonight, even something like ibuprofen, aspirin, Benadryl, or any alcohol — even a little bit?
No, nothing else, I swear. Just the Tylenol. I didn't have any alcohol or anything like that — I don't really drink much anyway. It was just the Tylenol.
Do you have any history of liver problems, or do you drink alcohol regularly, like on a weekly basis?

No, nothing like that. My liver's always been fine as far as I know, and like I said I don't really drink — maybe like once in a blue moon at a party or something, but not regularly. No liver problems or anything.

diagnosis: Acute acetaminophen overdose — toxic ingestion requiring NAC (N-acetylcysteine) therapy, pending Rumack-Matthew nomogram risk stratification
reasoning: The history is essentially confirmatory here: known intentional ingestion of ~10,000mg acetaminophen approximately 6 hours ago in a young male with no significant liver history. The acetaminophen level drawn at or after 4 hours post-ingestion will be plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to determine whether he falls in the treatment zone for NAC. LFTs are critical to establish a baseline — at 6 hours they may still be normal even with significant ingestion, but rising AST/ALT over the next 24-48 hours would indicate hepatocellular injury. Coagulation studies (PT/INR) are important because coagulopathy is a marker of hepatic synthetic dysfunction and would indicate progression toward acute liver failure. I ordered the salicylate level and urine drug screen because co-ingestion can change management significantly and patients sometimes underreport — ruling those out is important even if the history sounds clean.

patientInfo.name: Dmitri Voloshyn
patientInfo.chiefComplaint: Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain after taking 'a lot of Tylenol'

caseData.hpi: Dmitri Voloshyn is a 19-year-old male with no significant past medical history who presents to the emergency department approximately 6 hours after intentionally ingesting approximately 20 tablets of acetaminophen 500mg (total estimated dose ~10,000mg) following a breakup. He reports nausea, vomiting twice, and diffuse dull abdominal pain since shortly after ingestion. He denies co-ingestion of alcohol or other substances. He states he regrets the ingestion and is cooperative with treatment. He has no prior suicide attempts and denies current suicidal ideation.
differentials: Ingestion of another hepatotoxin (e.g., iron, mushroom)
keyQuestions: Exactly how many tablets were taken and at what time was the ingestion?
Were any other substances ingested concurrently (alcohol, opioids, other medications)?
Does the patient have any underlying liver disease, malnutrition, or chronic alcohol use that increases hepatotoxicity risk?
What is the patient's current mental health status and is psychiatry consultation needed?
teachingPoints: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is the gold standard for risk stratification in acute acetaminophen overdose — it plots serum acetaminophen level against time post-ingestion (at least 4 hours post-ingestion) to determine need for N-acetylcysteine (NAC) therapy. A level drawn too early (before 4 hours) cannot be plotted and may underestimate true peak.

N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is most effective when given within 8 hours of ingestion and restores hepatic glutathione, preventing toxic NAPQI accumulation. It should be started empirically while awaiting lab results if ingestion timing is concerning.

Early acetaminophen toxicity (0-24 hours) may present with only mild nausea and vomiting — LFT elevations (AST/ALT) typically appear in Phase II (24-72 hours), with peak hepatotoxicity in Phase III (72-96 hours). A near-normal initial LFT panel does NOT rule out significant toxicity.

Risk factors for increased hepatotoxicity include chronic alcohol use, malnutrition, fasting states, and concurrent use of CYP2E1 inducers (e.g., isoniazid, rifampin), which enhance conversion of acetaminophen to toxic NAPQI.
All patients with intentional overdose require mandatory psychiatric evaluation prior to discharge, regardless of medical stability.

components.name: Serum Acetaminophen
components.referenceRange: Plotted at 6 hours post-ingestion: treatment line threshold is ~150 mcg/mL at 4h, ~75 mcg/mL at 8h (Rumack-Matthew nomogram; reference range is time-dependent — this value at 6h falls in the HIGH RISK treatment zone)

imagingResults.Chest X-Ray: PA and lateral chest radiograph demonstrates clear lung fields bilaterally with no consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size. Mediastinal contour is unremarkable. Impression: No acute cardiopulmonary process.
imagingResults.Abdominal Ultrasound: Liver is normal in size with homogeneous echogenicity. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation; common bile duct measures 3mm. Gallbladder without stones or wall thickening. Spleen is normal. No ascites. Impression: No acute sonographic abnormality to suggest alternative hepatic pathology.

hiddenHistory.fullHistory: No significant past medical history. No prior hospitalizations or surgeries.
hiddenHistory.socialHistory: College freshman. Denies regular alcohol use. No tobacco. No illicit drug use. Broke up with long-term girlfriend yesterday. Lives in university dormitory. No travel history.
hiddenHistory.familyHistory: No family history of liver disease, metabolic disorders, or psychiatric illness.
hiddenHistory.medications: No chronic medications. Took approximately 20 tablets of acetaminophen 500mg (Extra Strength Tylenol) approximately 6 hours ago — total estimated dose 10,000mg in a single acute ingestion.
hiddenHistory.hiddenSymptoms: Patient admits to right upper quadrant tenderness on deep palpation if specifically asked; also reports diaphoresis and pallor noted by nursing staff shortly after arrival.

workingDiagnosis: Acute acetaminophen (Tylenol) overdose with potential hepatotoxicity risk
clinicalImpression: This is pretty straightforward on the surface — 19-year-old who tells us exactly what he took and how much. Around 10,000mg of acetaminophen is well above the toxic threshold, which I think is roughly 150mg/kg or more depending on the nomogram. He's 6 hours out from ingestion, which is actually a critical window because the Rumack-Matthew nomogram uses the 4-hour level to stratify risk. Right now he's in what I believe is Phase 1 — nausea, vomiting, malaise — which can look deceptively mild before potential liver injury sets in around 24-72 hours. I'm not too worried about an alternative diagnosis here given the clear history, but I want to make sure I'm not missing a co-ingestion.

grading.feedback: Excellent diagnostic performance — you correctly identified acute acetaminophen toxicity, appropriately referenced the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, and ordered a comprehensive, targeted workup including acetaminophen level, LFTs, coagulation studies, BMP, and a urine drug screen to rule out co-ingestions. Your interview efficiently covered timing, co-ingestion, and hepatic risk factors, which are the three most management-critical domains in this case. The main gap was not explicitly asking about psychiatric safety or requesting a psychiatry consult, which is mandatory in any intentional overdose regardless of medical stability.
strengths: Correctly identified the diagnosis and immediately referenced NAC therapy and the Rumack-Matthew nomogram for risk stratification — demonstrating strong foundational toxicology knowledge
Ordered a complete core workup: serum acetaminophen level, LFTs, coagulation panel, BMP, and urine drug screen — all appropriate and collectively sufficient to guide management
Efficiently confirmed single acute ingestion timing (6 hours post-ingestion), which is critical for correct nomogram plotting and NAC initiation window
Directly asked about co-ingestion of alcohol and other substances, appropriately ruling out factors that would alter hepatotoxicity risk and management

historyInterview.feedback: You covered the three highest-yield domains (ingestion timing, co-ingestions, and underlying liver disease/alcohol use) efficiently and directly; the notable gap was not asking about the patient's current mental health status, suicidal ideation, or the need for psychiatry consultation — a management-critical step in any intentional overdose.

testOrdering.feedback: The ordered panel — serum acetaminophen level, LFTs, coagulation studies, BMP, and urine drug screen — constitutes a complete and appropriate workup for acute acetaminophen toxicity with no unnecessary or harmful tests included.

diagnosisAccuracy.feedback: The submitted diagnosis of 'Acute acetaminophen overdose — toxic ingestion requiring NAC therapy, pending Rumack-Matthew nomogram risk stratification' is clinically accurate, correctly identifies the core pathological entity, and adds clinically supported and relevant specificity; this is fully correct and demonstrates strong clinical reasoning for a Foundations-level student.

diagnosisCompleteness.feedback: The core diagnosis is correctly and completely named; at Foundations level this is fully sufficient, and the mention of NAC and nomogram stratification adds meaningful clinical context, though the intentional/psychiatric dimension of the overdose was not explicitly addressed in the diagnosis statement.

missedQuestions: What is the patient's current mental health status — does he have active suicidal ideation, and has psychiatry been consulted? (Mandatory in all intentional overdoses regardless of medical stability and directly changes disposition planning)
teachingPoints: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is the gold standard for risk stratification in acute acetaminophen overdose — it plots serum acetaminophen level against time post-ingestion (at least 4 hours post-ingestion) to determine need for N-acetylcysteine (NAC) therapy. A level drawn too early (before 4 hours) cannot be plotted and may underestimate true peak.

N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is most effective when given within 8 hours of ingestion and restores hepatic glutathione, preventing toxic NAPQI accumulation. It should be started empirically while awaiting lab results if ingestion timing is concerning.

Early acetaminophen toxicity (0-24 hours) may present with only mild nausea and vomiting — LFT elevations (AST/ALT) typically appear in Phase II (24-72 hours), with peak hepatotoxicity in Phase III (72-96 hours). A near-normal initial LFT panel does NOT rule out significant toxicity.

Risk factors for increased hepatotoxicity include chronic alcohol use, malnutrition, fasting states, and concurrent use of CYP2E1 inducers (e.g., isoniazid, rifampin), which enhance conversion of acetaminophen to toxic NAPQI.
All patients with intentional overdose require mandatory psychiatric evaluation prior to discharge, regardless of medical stability.
differentials: Acute viral hepatitis: Could cause nausea, vomiting, and RUQ pain with transaminase elevation, but would not explain an acutely elevated serum acetaminophen level and onset would typically be more gradual without a clear ingestion history.
Intentional salicylate (aspirin) overdose: Important co-ingestion to exclude given overlapping GI symptoms; ruled out here by a normal serum salicylate level and negative urine salicylate screen.

Acute alcoholic hepatitis: Can cause nausea, vomiting, and transaminase elevation, but requires a history of chronic/heavy alcohol use — excluded here by history and a negative serum/urine ethanol screen.

Ingestion of another hepatotoxin (e.g., iron, amanita mushroom, carbon tetrachloride): Could cause acute hepatotoxicity, but the clear history of acetaminophen ingestion, confirmed serum level, and lack of supporting exposure history make these diagnoses unlikely.
